# **2026 – LinkedIn posts**

## **Wed 11 Feb – best rainbow ever**

What's your favourite means of transport?

If I had any say in it, I'd tell you: train, all the way, and everywhere.

I live on the southern coast of Spain, in the extremely pleasant city of Valencia.

And on top of the easy life, the trains. The local rail network is great around here: cheap, fast, reliable, clean, and comfortable.

But we also have low-cost high speed trains, and those are truly a thing of wonder.

Not only can you be in Madrid in less than 2 hours (rather than the 4 it would take in the car): it can also cost you less than 20 euros. I simply love it.

Back in November 2025 I was on a train to Madrid, on my way to the @Wey Wey Web conference in Málaga, where I was going to give a talk on accessibility as the best creative brief, as well as a workshop on colour.

I love that journey and almost wish it lasted longer. There's specifically one moment that particularly moves me, when the train flies by a huge reservoir surrounded by dramatic hills. I was working on my slides, but made sure I raised my eyes at that point.

And lo and behold: the full arc of the best rainbow I'd ever seen was crossing my favourite landscape. No way to make it justice with a phone-taken photo: you'll have to take my word for it. It was magnificent.

But if that weren't enough: the slide on my screen was the one about the visible colour spectrum (also known as the rainbow).

You couldn't make it up.

And that, my friend, marked the end of a really rather testing period and the beginning of a much easier one.

I love to think that the leap happened there, just past Chiva, when my screen matched the sky.

I'm back here, if you'll have me.

Image 1: A huge rainbow on top of a hill by a reservoir, seen from inside a train.

Image 2: A computer sitting on a table in a train compartment by the window, with a slide on the visible colour spectrum on its screen. Outside the window, a huge rainbow crosses the landscape. The person sitting opposite is looking out at the amazing natural display. 

## **Monday 16 Feb 2026 – NO\! Thanks**

Say "yes" more.

There are tribes and a movement built on these three words. It's a way for people to connect with more positivity in their lives. Which can be great, for sure.

But you know what's also very good to be able to do, when the situation calls for it?

Say "NO Thanks\!"

Loud and clear.

So if you object to giving your hard-earned money to companies that fund the vaporisation of civilians; the bombing of schools and hospitals; land theft; destruction of ancient trees and civilians' homes; imprisonment of thousands and thousands of civilians without clear cause, due process, or trial; torture and rape of such illegally detained civilians; calling for "volunteer executioners" of same illegally detained civilians; bombing and invasion of neighbouring countries; apartheid; total disregard and mockery of international law – and I could carry on...

then you should download the "No\! Thanks" app from whichever app store you favour.

This nifty little app allows you to know which brands are ethically safe to spend your money on, or not.

You can either scan the barcode when you do your shopping; or you can search for a brand you're thinking of buying from, and check out whether they're into financing war crimes, or not.

(Consider also that the type of company who thinks it's OK to drop white phosphorus on refugee camps, usually also underpays their staff, practices tax avoidance, favours authoritarian rulers, and all that jazz.)

The app "No\! Thanks" is non-profit, run by volunteers: you can choose whether to help it out with your donation, or not. It is otherwise entirely free to use.

( Yes I have notes on some UX and accessibility points and will see if I can relay them).


If you're thinking that this is only a drop in the ocean: remember that a large factor in the ending of apartheid in South Africa was international pressure, cultural isolation, and normal people like you and me saying "No\! Thanks" to companies supporting apartheid. (There already are actual consequences: for instance, 400 Starbucks stores recently shut in the US, with backlash from activists listed as one of the reasons).

If you're also thinking that what is happening in the Middle East is too far away to have anything to do with you: a country that systematically makes a mockery of the very international laws that were set up to guarantee its existence, with zero consequences, puts into grave peril any safety and human rights we've been enjoying in Europe since the end of World War 2\. And yeah, very selfishly, I am terrified.

Image description: The No\! Thanks app logo, in white all caps on a bright green background.

## **Tue 17 Feb 2026 – Bad news first**

Bad news first:

WCAG compliance does not mean actual accessibility.

Huge bummer innit\!

\[WCAG is the acronym for Web Accessibility Compliance Guidelines\]

Here's a few reasons why:

– WCAG do not consider \*all\* accessibility issues.

– WCAG do not consider all best practices. 

– WCAG do not consider overall inclusion.

– WCAG do not consider usability.

And here are a few examples of bad practices that cause accessibility issues and that are ignored by the WCAG:

– Use of low-contrast typefaces

– Use of complicated layouts

– Use of unfamiliar patterns

– Use of more than one H1

– Use of too many emojis

– Use of autoplay video\*

\*(no, it’s not enough to provide a stop button, or to make it stop after 5 seconds as the WCAG recommend. I only need 2-3 seconds to get sick. Please no autoplay – and no GIFs in emails, I can't stop them and won't be able to read your missive)

My point being: there is a big difference between providing a technically and legally compliant service, and providing a service that is truly accessible and inclusive.

Stop asking the question “does it comply with WCAG?” 

Start asking the question “can everybody use this?”

OK, that was the bad news.

And here's the brilliant news\!

Adopting inclusion will expand your business (and, why not, your karma too). And not by a little: by a hell of a lot.

Don't believe me? I've got proof\!

I've compiled a doc with recent research and data making the business case for accessibility, and demonstrating how accessibility is an unbelievably sound, long-term business strategy and growth driver – in bags of money as well as good karma.

Go get your copy here:

https://piccianeri.com/business-case-accessibility/

It may well be what you need to finally persuade your marketing department, or that of your clients, that they should embrace accessibility for better reasons than fear of legal trouble.

(Having said that though: legal trouble may well come your way – the European Accessibility Act is a biter – ask French online retail giants Auchan, Carrefour, Leclerc and Picard – but that's for another post\!)

In the meantime, please rest your eyes on this riotous cluster of gloriously pink flowers – for no other reason than: they're gorgeous, and you deserve it. Photo taken in Hackney Downs, on my way to my studio in Dalston, London, probably around 2013\.

Image description: close-up on multi-petalled, very fat, very pink cherry tree flowers on a bokeh background, that is to say, blurry green with large dollops of light.

## **Fri 20 Feb – arreggi the dogs**

Deleted as it immediately tanked – and used it to email the list

"Arreggi the dogs\!"

Immortal words pronounced by Roberto Benigni in the unforgettable 80s cinema classic Down by Law. This shining example of Tuscanglish (Tuscan \+ English) means:

"Hold off the dogs\!"

Which in Benigni's case were actual dogs, while in our case it's the virtual dogs that might be unleashed by EU legislation relative to the European Accessibility Act (EAA).

Yes because this is a phrase I keep hearing:

“The design and marketing staff are keen to learn and implement accessibility, but management aren’t sold on it yet”.

 

I usually say that I'd rather lead with the positive than use fear-mongering tactics, but...

Seriously?

What is it going to take for management to hold the dogs off – before they do bite them in the arse?

You've heard me many times before and here I go again, like a broken record, reminding you if you are the boss, and reminding you to remind your bosses if you’re not the boss, that the EAA is already implemented in all member states, and it might very well bite you, too, in the arse – if you ignore it, instead of taking action (or spending all your money on AI...).

Example: the on-going legal case against French online retail giants Auchan, Carrefour, Leclerc and Picard.

These are 3 simple questions you need to answer, in order to find out whether the EAA applies to you – or your clients:

• Does the company sell online in the EU?

• Is the company’s annual revenue over 2 million euros?

• Does the company have more than 10 employees?


If you’ve answered “yes” to all 3 of the questions above, but the company doesn’t have an accessibility program yet, or you haven't performed any accessibility audits on your digital properties: we need to talk. (And management doesn’t really have an option any longer).

Should you need a reminder, as well as ammunition with your C-Suite, please get my handy guide to the EAA:

https://piccianeri.com/eaa/

The guide will help you check whether your company falls under any of the excluded categories – and you can find more info on the French legal case I mentioned above, in case your C-suite needs that kind of kick up the arse.

If it doesn’t: I can empower you to make sure the EAA – and accessibility – are an opportunity, and not a limitation, or even, let's be honest, a massive, biting pain in the arse. Get into my messages, now.

(P.s: Down by Law, directed by Jim Jarmusch and starring Benigni, Tom Waits, and John Lurie, was released in Italy under the title Daunbailò, as Italian is a phonetic language and that's how we'd write it.)

Image description: The open mutt of a very scary dog with fangs out and red eyes, ready to bite, jumping at you so fast that its ears are blurry.

Saturday 21 Feb 2026 – lack of pay transparency

99% of job adverts on @LinkedIn and on most hiring platforms are sexist and anti-diversity.

Every single time I see an interesting job advert, it hardly ever includes the salary range.

So I don’t apply.

Why?

Because lack of pay transparency is the biggest red flag about a potential employer.

It usually means that:

– They don’t value equity

– They don’t value honesty

– They don’t value diversity

– They don’t value transparency

– They don't value experience

– They don’t want workers to unionise

– They don’t care about the gender pay gap 

– They plan to bag a bargain, not attract talent

– They’re ignoring EU/ US rules on pay transparency.

I honestly can’t believe that in 2026 we’re still having to discuss this.

We work for money.

Applying for a job takes days.

If I’m going to spend the best part of a week tailoring my CV and preparing my application, you need to tell me first off how much money you’re going to give me.

I give you my decades of experience, my skills, my talents, my fantastic personality.

What do you give me in return?

“Competitive salary” ain’t gonna hack it.

And when you claim that you have a "Commitment to Diversity and Inclusion" and swear that you are "fostering an inclusive environment" (looking at you, @Proton – I thought you were the good guys?)) but at the same time you are asking me to name my price – instead of being honest about your budget, and how much you value the role – well, then that really gets my goat.

It is a proven fact that women and persons from an underprivileged background will automatically value themselves much less than other categories.

Are you out to screw us?

Hell, this makes me so pi\*\*ed off, that 2.5 years ago I wrote a whole, long article about it for @Post Status . And unfortunately it is still as relevant now as it was then.

The extensive research I carried out for the article proved that when companies hide information on salary ranges, they are ALWAYS doing it for their own advantage. They are NEVER doing it for the sake of the candidate and existing employees.

You, reading this post.

Do you really want to work for a company that is planning to screw you before you’ve even met them?

You, reading this post.

Do you really want your candidates to know you're planning to screw them before you've even met them?

Here’s my article: https://poststatus.com/salary-transparency-why-not/

EU legislation info: https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/policies/pay-transparency/

US legislation info:

https://www.rippling.com/blog/pay-transparency-laws-state-by-state-guide

\#paytransparency \#payequity \#dei \#diversity \#diversityandinclusion \#genderpaygap

## **Monday 23 Feb – arreggi the dog**

"Arreggi the dog\!"

 

In Berlin, late December 1986, you could go see a film in the heart of the night – perfect for a young insomniac such as I was.

 So it was around 2 in the morning on an icy winter night when I first heard the legendary phrase:

 

"Arreggi the dog\!"

 

Uttered by Roberto Benigni, an Italian comedian from Tuscany, in the unforgettable 80s cinema classic Down by Law.

 

This shining example of Tuscanglish (Tuscan \+ English) means:

 

"Hold off the dogs\!"

 

Which in Benigni's case were actual police dogs. 

 

But in our case it's the virtual dogs that might be unleashed by EU legislation relative to the European Accessibility Act (EAA).

 

Yes because this is a phrase I still hear:

 

“The design and marketing staff are keen to learn and implement accessibility, but management aren’t sold on it yet”.   I usually say that I'd rather lead with the positive than fear, but...   Seriously?

 

What is it going to take for management to hold the dogs off – before they do bite them in the arse?

 

You've heard me many times before and here I go again: the EAA is already implemented in all member states, and it might very well bite you, too, in the arse – if you ignore it, instead of taking action.

 

Example: the on-going legal case against French online retail giants Auchan, Carrefour, Leclerc and Picard.

 

These are the 3 simple questions you need to answer, in order to find out whether the EAA applies to you – or your clients:

 

• Does the company sell online in the EU?

• Is the company’s annual revenue over 2 million euros?

• Does the company have more than 10 employees?   

If you’ve answered “yes” to all 3 of the questions above, but your company doesn’t have an accessibility program yet, or you haven't performed any accessibility audits on your digital properties: you need to take action.

 

Should you need a reminder of what the EAA entails, as well as ammunition with your C-Suite, here's my handy guide:

 

https://piccianeri.com/eaa/

 

It will help you check whether your company falls under any of the exempted categories – plus, more info on the French legal case I mentioned above.

 

If your company doesn’t qualify for exemption, and you're worried: don't be. There are ways to make sure that the EAA – and accessibility – are an opportunity, and not a limitation, or even, let's be honest, a massive, biting pain in the arse.

 

In a nutshell: a way to "arreggi the dogs", and even turn them into your best friends.

Want to know how? Send me a DM and let's talk.

Image description: Still from the Jim Jarmusch film Down By Law: Roberto Benigni, an Italian with shaky English on the run from a US prison, roaming a mangrove swamp in Louisiana, with both arms in the air screaming "Arreggi the dog", to the sound of dogs barking in the background.

## **Wed 25 Feb 2026– Acronyms**

Brace yourselves for a sweeping generalisation:

Native English-speaking people are generally very fond of....

Acronyms.

They are ubiquitous in English, the language I speak and write 80% of the time. And yet, they can be a real mystery for non-native speakers.

When I first started making British friends, sometimes my new mates would use a cryptic language, only for the initiated.

"Let me know your eeteea", one said to me at some point in the very early 90s.

What on earth is an eeteea? This was pre-internet, remember, so I couldn’t ask Google. Turns out: ETA \= Estimated Time of Arrival.

I soon realised that if I wanted to sound like a native, I had to learn them all: ASAP, AWOL, FUBAR\*… or the real meaning of "See You Next Tuesday."

Post-internet the acronyms frenzy only got worse. There are SO many out in the wild now – everywhere: no getting away from them.

But you know what?

Acronyms are not accessible.

Why? Because not everyone knows what they mean. Assuming they do creates barriers. I, for one, often get my knickers in a twist with acronyms:

– I don’t remember them, unless they are pervasively obvious

– As the students in my accessibility course know: I can struggle to pronounce them. I am non-native, spelling is an acquired skill

– They’re in all caps: the letters dance around

– I forget them very easily.

All of the above is 100 times worse for people with dyslexia.

There's more.

– Acronyms can confuse. I recently saw "CVD" in an article about colour contrast. Turns out it meant "Colour Vision Deficiency." But Google’s top results say "Cardiovascular Disease."

– Acronyms, unexplained, assume knowledge. The new recruit, brilliant at their job, but with English as a 2nd language, may not know that EOD means you need the report by the end of the day.

– Acronyms can mislead. See former British PM (Prime Minister\! See what I did there?) David Cameron's hilarious acronym blunder. He was outed as signing off his messages with “LOL” for “Lots of Love”, when it means "Laugh Out Loud”. You can blame him for many things (don't get me started), but not for that mistake.

Here’s my acronym advice:

– Spell it out: at least the first time, or in an easy to access footnote.

– Don’t assume knowledge: unless it really is obvious, or you know your audience very well.

– Careful with all caps: screen readers read all caps letter by letter.

– Don’t be lazy: it doesn't take long to write "Best Regards" instead of BR.

Accessibility is also about clear and inclusive communication. Let’s stop assuming, and start making our writing accessible for everyone.

What about you?

Do you know of any other Cameron-level acronym blunders?

Oh and here's a beautiful cluster of gloriously purple Jacaranda flowers, on a blurry green background, to remind us all that spring is just around the corner.

\*\*ASAP \= As Soon As Possible; AWOL \= Absent Without Leave, FUBAR \= Fucked UP Beyond All Repair. See comments for See You Next Tuesday

## **3 March 2026 – recovering creative – Wey Wey Web talk**

I already said this a number of times:

I'm a recovering creative.

No, it doesn't mean that being a creative is an addiction you should overcome.

It means that I've stopped serving my own ego when using my creativity in my design work, and started serving others with it instead.

The question I used to I ask myself:

"Is this cool, mysterious, and beautiful enough?"

The question I now I ask myself:

"Is this clear, usable, and accessible enough?"

What people think is that the 2nd question means I don't care about creativity, imagination, and beauty anymore.

But I'm sorry peeps, those people are wrong.

Creativity loves a good brief: and accessibility is the best brief of all.

To say it with [Karl Groves](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/#):

"If you think accessibility necessitates sacrificing the visual appeal of your product, then you have a creativity problem, not an accessibility problem.

Edit: or maybe both."

Hard agree on this one.

Plus: who ever said that creativity is only about the visuals? There is SO much more to it than that.

Want to find out more on how accessibility is, in fact, the best creative brief?

Easy\! Go watch the talk "The Best Creative Brief" delivered in November 2025 at the wondrous [Wey Wey Web](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/#) conference last Autumn: it's on YouTube and it's free\!

And yes, the talk features a pink double-decker bus full of wild animals and driven by a ginger kitten named Ciro, against a pink sky, with butterflies, wild flowers, and glitter galore. There's even a gold blimp up in the sky\! Poster for the 24th BFI London Lesbian and Gay Film Festival (now called "Flare") all the way back in 2010\.

## **Monday 9 March 2026 – Overlays**

Recycled from email to list

Flattered to the bone, I burst into a loud and enthusiastic “Yes\!”

When I was a child at a birthday party, the mother of the birthday child told me that I was the Chosen One for a wonderful surprise game, with an amazing prize at the end.

I had to follow verbal instructions to get to the end of an obstacle course while blindfolded, listening to this woman's instructions, with dozens of children shouting at the top of their lungs.

Then the screams get frantic. The mother is urging me on, yelling Yes\! You're there\! Just one more step\!

I trusted this woman blindly.

That last step downwards plunged me, fully clothed, straight into a paddling pool full of water. 

The noise went even louder.

It was the sound of ignominy.

It was the sound of 30 children hysterically laughing at me.

They'd been anticipating this moment in a frenzied crescendo, leading to my planned burning humiliation.

Who was laughing?

Everyone but me.

This, my friends, is what Deceptive Design is.

On the web, what this woman did to gullible little me would be called Bait and switch.

The user sets out to do one thing, but a different, undesirable thing happens instead.

And this is exactly what accessibility overlays do.

Overlays are website widgets that provide visitors with options to modify the site's user interface.

This may seem like an application of the 2nd principle of Universal Design: Flexibility in use. 

The core idea of this principle, as I was telling the students of the Accessible Design and Content course the other week, is that people can use the design in ways that work for them.

Great, no? 

No. 

Overlays claim they will make your website accessible, and legally compliant: they dangle the carrot telling you they will solve your accessibility issues, with zero effort on your part. 

It isn’t true.  

Overlays can often make it harder – when not downright impossible – for users of assistive tech to access the UI. 

In 2025, UsableNet reported that over 1,000 businesses using an overlay were sued for web accessibility violations. 

In Jan 2025, accessiBe was fined 1 million dollars by the US Federal Trade Commission, as compensation to their users who’d been duped into believing their accessibility was sorted – only to get sued by their own users. 

The overlay UserWay was also sued last year, for similar reasons. 

In the EU, German auditors have declared that the use of accessibility widgets disqualifies websites from accessibility certifications. 

In a nutshell:

If your company, or your company’s clients, use an overlay, or express the intention of doing so: tell them NO. 

Overlays are among the worst perpetrators of bait and switch ever. 

(at least as bad as that mean woman who tricked me into public indignity).  

Image: A carrot dangling from a string against a bright blue sky.

p.s. \!srael has been bombing hospitals, schools, and vital civilian infrastructure in \!ran. They did it in Gaza too. Please speak up this time.


P.s. Hilariously, accessiBe’s homepage features an unstoppable animation: a blatant failure of [WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 2.2.2 (Pause, Stop, Hide)](https://piccianeri.com/?ns_url=25gn&mid=151810&fch=66ccdbc5).


## **11 March – No looking back**

No looking back.

Once you get started, you can't stop.

A few years ago \[be honest Piccia: it was way more than a few\], I made the shift from being a creative designer to focusing fully on UX.

I threw myself into the world of user journeys, personas, microcopy, and everything else that makes a digital experience feel seamless – increasing conversions as the cherry on the cake.

I was all in. Obsessed with delivering the best experience possible.

And then I came across accessibility.

And it very soon dawned on me: there is no such thing as good UX if a portion of your audience – whatever the size – is excluded by design.

If someone can’t read the text, can’t use a keyboard to navigate, can't know the content of an essential image for lack of alt text, or is stuck waiting for a screen reader to plough through ten pointless links before getting to the main content: then the experience is not just bad.

It’s broken. For everyone, regardless of ability.

That realisation changed everything. It changed the way I design, the way I think, and the way I teach.

So now, accessibility isn’t an add-on.

It actually is the user experience.

And once you start seeing it like that, there is no looking back.

That's why everything I do now, is from an accessibility-first point of view.

There can be no looking back.

## **Mon 16 March – Gen Z and captions**

I never had children and I live on a roof, now without even a cat.

That means that I no longer fall right into the category of childless cat lady, which is a shame as it was part of my identity, plus I was in excellent company (Taylor Swift, just to mention one).

However, even though I have no cats and no children, rooftops in a sea town tend to be nice all year round: as a result, mine is still often swarming with a variety of young people taking in the rays, who apparently don't need the allure of a cat to come spend time with me.

My niece and nephew, their partners, and their posses at large, plus my UK godchildren who also seem to have a vast entourage with discerning tastes (not all at the same time, thankfully).

So despite my own \[planned\] lack of offspring, I am often surrounded by a multitude of Gen-Z from all over the place.

Perhaps I'm lucky, but they're all great (which means their Gen-X parents, i.e. my generation, didn't do such a bad job after all).

Yes, I think Gen-Z are brilliant: and we have a lot of things in common. Just a few essential ones here:

* We have similar taste in music (they like vintage so that's me)  
* We have a very strong sense of justice and ethics  
* We swap size 38 All Stars, Doc Martens, and Birkenstocks all the time (mine only black flip-flops though); as well as hoodies and other items of clothing, and:  
* We always turn closed captions on when we watch a show.

Yes. Apparently about 80% of Gen-Z use closed captions, even though their hearing is perfect. My niece and nephew started because they were encouraged to watch shows in the original language. As a result, their English is excellent now. Then, they continued the habit because they find it helps with cognition.

Just like me, then\! Who would have thought.

Captions really are one of the best examples of an accessibility feature that is essential for some, as well as incredibly useful for all. 

Captions are also an accessibility feature that most of us who post anything online need to know how to use.

So here's how you can learn more about captions, and make sure that everyone can equally enjoy your video content online: watch me talk about them and show how to do it right\!

We Need to Talk About Captions

https://equalizedigital.com/we-need-to-talk-about-captions-piccia-neri/

(Yes, I do issue a carpet ban on those dreadful, sick-making fancy captions effects)

Thank you to @Amber Hinds and the team for inviting me last year to talk about my favourite accessibility feature to demonstrate the "curb cut effect": an accessibility-driven innovation that ends up benefitting everyone.

Image description: The terrace I live on, the one that Gen-Z love, when the orange cat Ciro was still living here. Much-missed Ciro is on the sofa and his face is close to the camera, backlit by the evening sun, with the terrace plants in the background.

## **Wed 18 March – No centred text**

I want to live in a world where...

... Nobody ever centres their text.

I know that there are bigger issues at stake in the world right now.

But for a start, I am asking you to please, not centre your text. Ever.

Better said: you can centre text on a wedding invitation, or on a tombstone, or a book cover.

Any other time you feel tempted, ask yourself:

"Is this a wedding invitation?"

"Is this a book cover?"

"Is this a tombstone?"

If the answer is "no", then:

❌ Do not centre your text.

Centred text is basically not aligned: a usability & accessibility issue.

Every time a line ends there's a lost moment as the reader spends precious time locating the beginning of the next.

With no clear edge to focus on, the cognitive load increases.

The visitor works harder to focus on where to go next.

People with vision issues have an even harder time.

So do people with neuro-atypical issues such as dyslexia and/ or autism.

People like me who see shapes instead of text: sometimes it's a UFO, at other times a vase, or a hat...

✅ Do this instead:

For a Western language, align left. Your visitors' eye will fall there first: make use of that.

Don't be tempted to justify: it causes unsightly gaps and cognitive delays.

Think of content structure: is this the title of the page, and therefore an h1? Using correct HTML hierarchy will help screen readers scan your page & make sense of the content. This is a great SEO tip, on top of accessibility.

Use text size and weight to create visual hierarchy and lead the eye of your visitors to where you want it. Use the power of design\!

Use colour sparingly, never to convey crucial meaning or differentiation.

Use a contrast checker, always.

Follow this advice: you'll automatically convert more.

There is no way I can back this wild claim, but I know it's true.

Recapping:

1: Left-align your text, and use alignment in the layout.

2: Ragged lines, never justified.

3: Correct HTML structure for SEO & accessibility.

4: Visual hierarchy for better usability.

5: Always use a contrast checker.

Image description: Slide 1 shows the “Piccia Neri” logo on the top left. In the centre: a block of white text centred on a black background with each line beginning randomly. It says:"Centred alignment is basically lack of alignment. It looks messy and untidy. The eye has nowhere to anchor.

The lines are ragged in an unpredictable way. It is disorienting and hard to read for neuro-divergent people, those with low vision issues, and old type nerds like me." Below the text block: emoji face crying rivers. Slide 2: same block of text in white on black, aligned to the left. Below the text block: emoji face with a wide smile.

\[I do more than anything wish I could live in a world where rogue countries such as \!srael are not allowed to bomb and invade their neighbours, too, but while I can't do that, I can remind you that it's wrong, illegal, inhumane, and what will it take to stop them??\]

## **Friday 20 March 2025 – empty list**

Here’s a list of accessibility issues that I’m not affected by, because I don’t identify as disabled:

1\. …

2\. …

3\. …

4\. …

5\. …

6\. …

….

Yes that’s right: it’s an empty list.

Because the actual percentage of (potentially) disabled people in the world is 100%. It definitely includes me – with the recent memory of my right arm immobilised in a sling still vividly fresh, as it's left permanent consequences: navigating life, and the web, is way harder when you're down a limb.

This percentage definitely includes you. And also all of your customers and site visitors, bar none.

After all, as Cindi Li was probably the first to say, we’re only ever temporarily abled.

Have you ever broken a limb? Or had conjunctivitis? Or a blocked ear? Or a child in your arms? Or the sun in your eyes?

“Over 1 billion people around the world live with some form of disability. It is estimated that one in five people have a permanent disability, but 100% of the population will be faced with vision, hearing, motion, or cognitive disabilities at some point in their lives.” 

(by Sara Soueidan).

Yes. A hundred per cent includes me, and it also includes you.

So when you next think “What’s in it for me?” before you invest in accessibility, here’s your answer: besides increasing your current reach, you’re also investing in a possible future you.

Design for your future self. And for the present selves of billions of people, who need you to be thinking about them, too.

For this reason, accessibility is one of the best marketing tools available to us: check the comments for a document I compiled, providing you with abundant proof that you, too,  can turn accessibility into an opportunity, instead of a perceived limitation.

Image description: the last slide in my talk “Dizzy users close browsers”, with the famous quote from Sir Tim Berners-Lee, the father of the World Wide Web: “The power of the Web is in its universality. Access by everyone regardless of disability  is an essential aspect”.

You can watch the talk on the UX Copenhagen YouTube channel, or on the beyond tellerrand Berlin 2023 page.

\#DontBeADick \#BeAccessible

## **Monday 23 March 2026 – accessible links**

A few years ago, I vowed to stop being a dick online and embraced accessibility.

And no:

– Accessibility doesn’t make the web “boring”

– Accessibility doesn’t mean “not creative”

– Accessibility doesn’t bring limitations

– Accessibility is the main ingredient of good UX

When you embrace accessibility, your world opens up hugely.

Take for instance non-descriptive links. 

You know, like the bog-standard “Read more” in most article archive pages.

This seemingly innocuous invite is a frustrating mystery for those using assistive tech. (And yes, you, too, have visitors that use screen readers or other assistive technologies).

People who can’t scan a web page or an app by reading it, can decide to use links (or headings) to find out what’s in it.

(This includes loads of people who are not permanently disabled, by the way: a lot more people than you think use screen readers, or voice activated tech, or navigate a page with tabs.)

When your links are:

– “Click here”

– “Read more”

– “Go to this page”

Et similia, without any indication of content or context, many people (approx 25% of your audience) will have no idea where that link will end up.

I’m not going to “Click here”, if I don’t know where the click will take me.

In the same vein, when you have dozens of “Read more” on an archive page full of articles, you can imagine the frustration they might cause.

Why take the time to write a piece and link to it, if you don’t make sure people will know what it’s about?

I certainly won’t “Go to the page”, either, if I have no idea why I should bother.

Do this instead:

– Make links more descriptive: “Explore \[company name\]’s web design services”

– Make the article title in the archive a clickable link, and get rid of “Read more” altogether

– Make it clear within the text what the contents of the page will be, and link to that instead: “You can buy \[the product\] here”.

Another positive side-effect? Of course, much better SEO juice.

Stop being a dick online. Embrace accessibility, and just like I did, you also will:

– Expand your audience massively

– Avoid fines and lawsuits 

– Earn masses of good karma

– Discover new professional opportunities you never thought existed.

Here's another post I wrote, with more tips on how to write descriptive links: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/piccia-neri\_accessibility-a11y-dontbeadick-activity-7253056305970483202-Vvwe?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop

And here's an article on accessible hyperlinks, too:

https://piccianeri.com/accessible-hyperlinks/

Image description: The aubergine emoji with a large red cross on top of it. Meaning in the context: let's all stop being dicks to disabled people.

**\#DontBeADick** **\#AccessibilityMatters** \#accessibility

## **Wednesday 25 March 2026 – accessible Word doc checklist**

Myth that needs to be debunked:

You can fix an inaccessible PDF after the fact.

Well, sure. You can add tags. You can manually set the reading order. You can spend a deeply tedious afternoon in Acrobat trying to untangle a document that was born without a thought to disabled people.

Or – and I cannot stress this enough how much better this approach is – you can do the work in Word before you export.

Yep. Just start with accessibility.

Because here's what happens when you export a Word document to PDF:

If your headings are real headings (using Word's built-in styles, not manually bolded text), they become tagged headings in the PDF.

If your images have alt text in Word, they carry that alt text through to the PDF.

If your tables have a proper header row marked as such, the PDF inherits that structure.

If your reading order in Word is logical: the PDF follows it.

But if none of the above "ifs" are true: the PDF will be, in the technical sense, a disaster. The tags will be wrong or absent. The reading order will be chaotic. A screen reader will read it in the order the elements were added to the document, which is almost never the order they appear on the page.

The moral of the story is not "learn Acrobat".

The moral is: do it right in Word. Export. Done.

Every single accessibility decision made upstream saves hours of remediation downstream.

And you know what? Not only is it free: it will also make you save heaps of time and money later.

Yes. For the "accessibility is expensive" brigade: making a Word document accessible costs exactly the same as making it inaccessible.

But making it inaccessible will cost you dearly, in time and in remediation money.

Don't know how? I've got you covered\! Today I'm sharing with you the same Accessible Word document checklist I give to my workshop clients. This will soon turn into a paid asset, so do grab it now.

[https://piccianeri.com/word-checklist/](https://piccianeri.com/word-checklist/)

## **Tuesday 31 March 2026 – Ageism in forms**

Not today perhaps: but one day.

I've told you before but it's happened again, just yesterday, so here I am, telling you once more.

One day, you, too, will be over 50\.

If you are already: you know that 50 is not the same as 55, which is not the same as 60, which is not the same as 65 – etc.

 

Up to possibly to our 90s: longevity rates are increasing, and we tend to keep much fitter than previous generations.

I am 58 (no idea how it happened, but still, way better than the alternative). I look after my body and take exercise, but I am categorically not the same as I was when merely a sprightly young 50\. 

I know that when I am 68, I'll look back nostalgically at today and I'll see a spring chicken in comparison.

So when a fitness app, called Nordletics because hell yeah name and shame, that's previously told me it is conceived to help menopausal and post-menopausal women, asks me to select my age, but shoves me together with all other 50+, with no further nuance...

I will not buy it or even try it, because it has just shown me for a fact that I can't trust it with my health.

If you lump everyone above 50 in the same bucket: you have no idea how to look after our health. 

This is ageist and against good UX: so why does it still happen? Why are we over 50s constantly dismissed – when we're one of the generations with the highest spending power?

While I can't know the answer for sure, here's a hypothesis: 

When these apps run A/B tests, the quantitative data don't yield significant results. 

So that's why I'm telling you: no, seriously, f\*\*\* quantitative data.

Joking: that's clearly over the top.

 

But there are cases where quantity or statistics definitely should NOT matter.

This is one of them. 

If it makes no difference: go qualitative, all the way.

Add those age ranges.

Do you know why?

Because we exist. And we're going nowhere yet.

Broken record, here it goes again: 

Design for your future self. Don't shove all 50+ in the same category: provide us with the same nuance everybody else gets.

(this is excellent accessible design advice in general, by the way)

By pissing us off with your thoughtless age ranges, you are absolutely leaving plenty of good money on the table.

Gen-X is re-defining what ageing means. 

We're climbing mountains, running marathons, smashing triathlons, skating, cycling, swimming, free diving, scuba diving, surfing, sailing, boxing, tangoing our way into our sunset years, and we have no intention to stop. 

Any health or fitness apps or organisations ignoring this, are wasting money by the fistful.

We're here to stay. You better acknowledge it, if you want our dosh.

Ageism is a thing: just don't do it.

Image description: 2nd screen of a quiz for the app Nordletics. The title is "Personalized plan to lose weight at home for women" and below, smaller "Based on your age". Below, 4 women images in a row, with an age range below each one: 18-29; 30-39; 40-49; and 50+.

![][image1]

## **Thursday 2nd April 2026 – Pizza – accessible word doc checklist**

A myth that needs to be debunked:

You can only get the best Italian pizza in Naples. 

Well, sure. You get fantastic pizza in Naples, also because mozzarella comes from southern Italy. So any pizza with fresh mozzarella on it has an unfair advantage. 

But the best Italian pizza I ever had was in London. Granted: the pizzeria was 100% Neapolitan, and they did have a wood oven of course.

I could carry on talking about pizza, but there's another myth I want to debunk today: 

You can fix an inaccessible PDF after the fact.

Well, sure. You can add tags. You can manually set the reading order. You can spend a deeply tedious afternoon in Acrobat untangling a document born without a thought to disabled people.

Or you can do the work in Word before you export.

Which I cannot stress enough is the better approach.

If you've been around for a while, you've heard this from me before: just start with accessibility.

Proposals, briefs, reports, guidelines, handover docs. We all write them. A lot of them end up as PDFs at some point, or HTML, if you're publishing to the web. 

Here's what happens when you export a Word document to PDF or HTML.

If your headings are real headings (not manually bolded text), they become properly tagged headings in the export. 

If your images have alt text in Word, they carry it through. 

If your tables have a proper header row, the structure is inherited. 

If your reading order in Word is logical, the export follows it.

But if none of the above is true: the output will be a disaster. 

The tags will be wrong or absent. The reading order will be chaotic. A screen reader will read it in the order the elements were added to the document, which is almost never the visual order.

The moral of the story is not "learn Acrobat", or "only have pizza in Naples". 

The moral, and this applies to everything, not just Word, is: do it right at the beginning. Be it pizza, or a digital document. 

Every single accessibility decision made upstream saves hours of remediation downstream. 

And it's free. Not only free: it will save you heaps of time and money later. 

Most "accessibility decisions" are in fact, very simply, best practice decisions. With this cherry tomato on the pizza: your content will be much clearer and easier to digest.

For the "accessibility is expensive" brigade: making a Word document accessible costs exactly the same as making it inaccessible. While making it inaccessible will cost you dearly, in time and  remediation money.

Don't know how to get Word right? 

I've got you covered. I've put together an Accessible Word document checklist: the same one I give my workshop clients. 

This will be a paid asset, part of a bigger suite, so now is the time to get it for free.

Just comment PIZZA on this post.

Image description: A tomato and mozzarella pizza ready to be eaten, sitting just out of the entrance to the wood oven, with the roaring fire inside the oven in the background.

![][image2]

## **Tuesday 7 April 2026 – WebAIM Million report 2026**

Focusing on improvement is part of human nature. 

We all want to get better at what we do: we all want our latest output to be better than the previous. Whether you're a writer, a barista, a musician, a chef, a teacher, a carpenter, a cleaner, a designer: whatever your field of human endeavour, it is human to want to keep producing better results as we evolve.

 

So when I tell you that web accessibility got measurably worse this year, it is definitely with a heavy heart and a crying rivers emoji. 

😭

 

Every year, WebAIM (Web Accessibility In Mind, an accessibility research organisation based at Utah State University) analyses the home pages of the top one million websites and checks them for accessibility errors. 

This year's results are the worst in recent memory. The average number of detectable errors per page has gone up by more than 10% in a single year: from 51 errors per page in 2025 to 56.1 in 2026\. The share of home pages with detected WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) failures has gone back up too, from 94.8% to 95.9%, reversing six consecutive years of slow but real improvement. 

😭😭😭

 

The most common failures are the same six as always: 

1\. low contrast text

2\. missing alt text

3\. missing form labels

4\. empty links

5\. empty buttons

6\. missing document language. 

These six categories account for 96% of all detected errors, and they have not changed since the study began in 2019\. 

Seven years. The same six problems. Still not fixed.

 

The report points to some probable causes: increased page complexity, over-reliance on third-party libraries... 

But the worst culprit is probably what we affectionately know as "vibe coding": using AI tools to generate code quickly, accepting the output, and shipping it without checking whether it actually works for everyone.

It's like a miracle\! Look, mum, I made an app in an afternoon\! 

 

The reality, too often, is a disastrous div soup with no semantic structure, no heading hierarchy, images with no alt text: the full catalogue of WebAIM's greatest hits, generated automatically and at scale.

 

I use Claude, and other vide coding tools, to help me design and build things. Of course. They've quickly become tools of the trade. But I know enough to brief them properly: I know what accessible HTML and design look like, I know to check the output, and I know when something has gone wrong.

 

That knowledge has to come from somewhere. Which is precisely why I do what I do (courses, workshops, in-house training, and consultancy). 

 

If there's anything we can learn from the WebAIM Million 2026, it's that we should all try to reverse the trend. So whether you want to learn with me or with someone else: let's keep vibe coding, but let's make sure we train the tools to do it with accessibility in mind.

 

Can we make that promise to ourselves?

## **Thursday 9 April 2026 – Fuck Israel**

I don't want to hear anyone ever say they are a Star Wars fan, or sing "Bella Ciao", and then support Israel. You are either against the ruthless invaders who act in flagrant defiance of any and all international laws, or you are with them. There is nothing in between. 

(Also, who would have thought anyone would ever have a nostalgic thought about Ronald Reagan. But such are the times we live in)  
Reposting this: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/tmhall99\_in-case-you-missed-it-israel-dropped-over-activity-7447988667991654401-59nD?utm\_source=share\&utm\_medium=member\_desktop\&rcm=ACoAAAJ3Vq8BvZZ1hun3XmgIIuFxEHRCumTvXeU

## **Tuesday 21 April 2026 – Alt text** 

3 things that you think will be easy, but aren't: 

– Electrical mountain bike (still quite a lot of skill and sweat required).

– Meditation (you just sit and think of nothing, right? right...).

– Alternative text for images.

Pesky alt text\! It seems like it should be easy, doesn't it? Just look at the image, and describe it. Then you try to do it in a real-world scenario, and it really isn't.

For example: a designer on my Accessible Design and Content course is working on a museum website redesign that must comply with WCAG (Web Content Accessibility Guidelines) Level AA. 

Their question: how long should alt text be for artworks? 

The WCAG guideline says "the most concise description possible", and the WAVE accessibility checker flags a warning above 100 characters.

But 100 characters cannot begin to describe a painting. No way\! As a trained art historian, I resent that. 

Let's clarify a few things, then. 

First: the 100-character limit in WAVE is a rough heuristic, not a rule. WCAG success criterion 1.1.1 specifies no character limit. "Concise" is relative to the complexity and purpose of the image.

Second: healthy reminder that automated checkers always need a manual check, WCAG are guidelines, and following them alone may give you false negatives, as well as false positives.

Third, and most importantly: another healthy reminder that, to be accessible and inclusive, we must always strive to give everyone the same experience, regardless of ability. 

For artworks, that means the alt text needs to actually describe the image. Think of it as describing a painting to someone over the phone, someone who cannot see it and is relying entirely on your words.

Take the example below: a landscape by Jean-François Raffaëlli, Montmartre in the snow (1880–1890), from the Museum of Bellas Artes in Bilbao.

A good alt text: "A painting of a snow-covered urban square in winter, almost entirely rendered in white, grey, and pale beige. Two bare trees dominate the foreground on the left, their dark branches reaching into an overcast sky. Small dark figures cross the open ground in the middle distance, and a corner building with snow-dusted rooftops closes the right side of the composition. The mood is quiet and still."

Enough to give someone a real sense of the image, but not overwhelmingly detailed (yet, most definitely above 100 characters\!). 

You want people who use screen readers to be able to “scan” the painting rapidly and get an idea, the way people with vision do. Screen reader users all have jobs\! Chores\! Stuff to do\! Let’s make sure they get the gist and the feeling of the image, but let’s not burden them with a super long description. 

On the other hand, the long description, which lives elsewhere on the page or in an expandable section, can go deeper into technique, palette, composition, and historical context.  

Is alt text getting easier for you? Or still a bit of a pain in the arse? Let me know in the comments\!

## **Friday 24 April 2026 – Train your staff**

With the Zichi photo. Recycled and slightly edited from the post on 10 Feb 2025 [https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:share:7294667820489973763/](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:share:7294667820489973763/) 

Want to save money – and make money – on accessibility?

Simple:

Train your staff.

This is a hill I'd happily die on: if you invest in accessibility training for your team now, you’ll spend a lot less fixing problems later.

And you will also open up your audience to a lot more people.

Here’s what happens when your team doesn’t know accessibility:

– They create inaccessible content

– (Perhaps just after that expensive audit and remediation investment\!)

– So the next scan returns accessibility issues

– You have to rely on expensive external help – again

– You waste time retro-fitting instead of doing it right the first time

– You risk legal trouble (trust me on this one – the European Accessibility Act is ON and so is accessibility legislation in the rest of the world)

– You shut out 25% of your audience – yes, that’s the percentage of people who have a disability at any given time (don't get me started on "we are all only ever temporarily abled" but that's the truth)

Here’s what happens when your team does know accessibility:

– They create accessible content from the start

– They learn how to self-assess and scan, so they can monitor their output

– You don’t have to call in expensive external help as often

– (Although of course, you regularly hire disabled testers)

– You reach a bigger audience and make more money

– You avoid legal headaches

– You build a better, more inclusive brand

– You turn accessibility into an opportunity – instead of a limitation

The choice is yours. But if you’re going to wait until you get sued, lose customers, or have to rebuild everything from scratch… my conscience will be clear, because I did warn you.

3 things you can do now:

– Download my handy guide to the European Accessibility Act, and check whether it applies to you or not: https://piccianeri.com/eaa/

– Buy The Accessible Design and Content course while it's still at the pre-sale price: https://piccianeri.com/adc/

– Send me a message to chat about how I can help you turn accessibility into an opportunity, by delivering bespoke trainings to your teams.

Happy Friday, by the way\!

And here's a fluffy white cat in a basket for the algo, and for your delectation. He's seen from above and is looking up at the camera with huge, and slightly concerned, blue eyes. There's a hint of orange in the light markings on his face, and on his coat. It's clear from the floof volume that he'd smell of talcum powder, should you bury your face in that gorgeous softness. We loved you Tsiki, we were so lucky to have you in our lives, you were the bestest animated plush toy ever.

## **Monday 27 April 2026 – alt text vs caption**

The equivalence principle: 

Give everyone the same experience, regardless of ability.

So if you have an image on a page, you must also describe it for those who can’t see it. And that’s alt text (see my post: https://lnkd.in/eAyK8HxP).

But what about captions though? 

Are they the same thing? 

If you write a caption, can you skip the alt text?

Sorry to disappoint: nope, alt text and captions are not the same thing, and they serve completely different purposes.

Alt text is for people who cannot see the image: screen reader users, people on slow connections, search engines.

Alt text is invisible in normal use. It lives in the document or page structure, it is read by screen readers, and it is displayed by browsers when an image fails to load. The rest of the time, nobody sees it (unless you’re on Bluesky, where you can click on the ALT tag on an image, and read the alt text). 

(That's why, by the way, I write an image description on my LI posts: not everyone with low vision uses a screen reader).

So, what's a caption for? 

A caption is visible text, placed near the image, read by everyone including screen reader users.

A caption adds something the image alone doesn't say: where it was taken, when, by whom, why it matters in the context, or other information of interest.

And here, my friends, is where most people get tangled up. 

When you write a caption that fully describes the image, repeating all of it in the alt text is actively annoying for screen reader users, who will hear the same information twice. 

And that's a misunderstanding of the role of a caption.

A caption assumes you know what’s in the image: it is meant to add further information that you cannot necessarily infer from the image itself. 

For instance, in the case of a painting, the caption should contain essential information, such as the author, the title, the year, and so on, that you wouldn't know just by looking at the image.

Or in the case of a portrait of former US First Lady Michelle Obama, the caption may say: "The US First Lady Michelle Obama in 2009, wearing the inaugural ball gown designed by Jason Wu. Obama became known for using her fashion choices to support young, diverse, and independent designers".

The difference between alt text and caption is in the top 5 questions I get asked. I hope this helps\! 

Do you have any other alt text or caption question? 

Image description: A painting of a snow-covered urban square in winter, almost entirely rendered in white, grey, and pale beige. Two bare trees dominate the foreground on the left, their dark branches reaching into an overcast sky. Small dark figures cross the open ground in the middle distance, and a corner building with snow-dusted rooftops closes the right side of the composition. The mood is quiet and still.

Caption: Jean-François Raffaelli, Montmartre sous la neige, Montmartre in the snow, 1880-1890, Museo de Bellas Artes, Bilbao, Spain.

## **Monday 1 June 2026 – ratios at tech events**

Happy Monday everyone, and let's talk about gender and ethnicity ratios at tech events.

The weekend before last I was speaking at a Tech Fest in my home city of Valencia, and that all went very well thank you very much, really enjoyed the experience.

The ratio in the attendance, roughly and by eyesight only, was 80 men to 20 women at best, 90% white.

Except, notably, the talk on women in tech as an untapped resource by the brilliant [Mariia Suleiman](https://www.linkedin.com/in/mariia-suleiman/), which ran a full inversion of that ratio, I'd say even 90 to 10\.

A ratio that shifted a little further after I went into the hallway and grabbed a few men-presenting folks by the scruff of the neck, figuratively of course, but with the same effect.

(Mate: WE already know. We need YOU in the room.)

This week I am speaking at [UX London](https://www.linkedin.com/company/ux-london/), so the other day I finally sat down and went through the speakers line-up properly. Strange that I hadn't done it sooner, as equity of representation is one of my criteria for acceptance when a conference invites me, but anyway.

Well knock me over with a feather.

What a slap in the face of every tech conference that claims it is simply not possible to have a truly representative line-up: multiple perspectives, multiple faces, multiple experiences, rather than the same default one we've all been staring at for decades (that's a white middle aged man in case you're wondering. I do love you, white middle aged man, but we've heard from you, and keep hearing from you. Time to hear from others, too).

Yes, my friend. It is possible. UX London has done it. The [Clearleft](https://www.linkedin.com/company/clearleft/) team has done it. Go look for yourself: [https://lnkd.in/eTJk8BDk](https://lnkd.in/eTJk8BDk)

I'm speaking on Design Day, tomorrow, Wednesday 3 June, on the intersection of creativity and accessibility. And I'm thrilled to be part of this line-up: even more so than I already was.

If you're coming, come and find me. If you're not, come next year\!

With many thanks once again to [Jeremy Keith](https://www.linkedin.com/in/adactio/) and [Louise Ash](https://www.linkedin.com/in/louiseash/) and everybody else in the UX London team.

Image description: The UX London 2026 website showing the glorious diversity of all nine Discovery day speakers for Tuesday 2 June, arranged in a grid of circular portrait photos on a dark navy background. The speakers are: Maria Isachenko, UX Researcher (talk, research); Melin Edomwonyi, UX Director at Seedable Studio (talk, strategy); Marley Dizney Swanson, Product Manager at Bolt Insight (talk, research); Luisa Berta, Senior Product Designer (talk, learning); Feyikemi Akinwolemiwa, Research and Design Manager at National Grid (workshop, strategy); Natasha den Dekker, Senior UX Researcher at Made Tech (workshop, research); Chris How, Head of Experience Design at Clearleft (workshop, IA); Lucrezia Ponzano, Collaboration Strategist and Facilitator (workshop, alignment); and Michael Kibedi, Design Researcher.

![][image3]

[image1]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image2]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image3]: <data:image/png;base64,>
